description: Conceptual diagram for 'The Coordination Problem in Long-Form Writing': Explain why books are difficult to manage when outlines, drafts, citations, diagrams, reviews, and builds drift apart.

Conceptual diagram for 'The Machine as a Codynamic System': Define the central thesis: the system works by keeping outline, agents, task queues, dependencies, artifacts, and compiled outputs evolving together.

Conceptual diagram for 'The Repository as the Case Study': Explain that the paper is grounded in the current repository, its YAML outlines, agent definitions, runtime logs, generated diagrams, and compiled LaTeX artifacts.
Conceptual diagram for 'Schema, Intent, and Metadata': Explain why the system captures purpose, audience, authorial voice, metadata, publication settings, and epistemic stance before generation.
Conceptual diagram for 'Depth Without Special Cases': Show how the same structural element supports chapter, section, and subsection nodes.
Conceptual diagram for 'Structural and Narrative Dependencies': Describe machine-readable dependency edges and human-readable narrative dependency notes.

Conceptual diagram for 'State Surfaces: Source, TeX, Logs, and Artifacts': Distinguish imported source material, generated section payloads, build outputs, runtime logs, media requests, and diagram memory as separate but coordinated state surfaces.
Conceptual diagram for 'Conversational Intake': Explain the question bank: title, purpose, audience, reader takeaway, voice, genre, design spec, and artifact structure.
Conceptual diagram for 'Outline Import and Normalization': Explain conversion from legacy YAML, markdown headers, numbered outlines, and raw text into canonical work YAML.
Conceptual diagram for 'Book Repository Layout': Describe outline files, source sections, TeX payloads, references, logs, build artifacts, media requests, diagram memory, and artifact registries.
Conceptual diagram for 'Agent Roles': Map the major agents to their responsibilities: hypervision, outlining, section drafting, gardening, references, diagrams, design, and assembly.

Conceptual diagram for 'Task Queues and Action Selection': Explain queued tasks, action ids, task context, priorities, generated prompts, status transitions, and how section agents choose which declared task to run.

Conceptual diagram for 'Messages, Callbacks, and the Inter-Agent Chat Log': Explain routed messages, process_message callbacks, self-messages, the readable chat log format, and the hypervisor's access to unprocessed chat lines.

Conceptual diagram for 'Introspective Starts': Describe how starting a section agent asks it to inspect the whole document, its section, the outline, references, and diagram opportunities, then queue a self-addressed plan.

Conceptual diagram for 'The Maintenance Cycle': Describe the gardener's recurring task set: inspect changes, compare sections against parent/siblings/dependencies/downstream uses, and queue targeted editorial work.

Conceptual diagram for 'Editorial Checks and Revision Requests': Separate repeated claims and thin transitions from missing definitions and unsupported assertions, and explain how each category routes to section agents or references_agent.
Conceptual diagram for 'Global Drift and Hypervisor Escalation': Explain how the gardener sends the hypervisor a list of affected section agents so the hypervisor can direct coordinated revisions.

Conceptual diagram for 'Reference Ownership and Citation Support': Explain why references_agent owns references/references.bib, how section agents verify registered keys, and how missing support is routed.

Conceptual diagram for 'Diagram Requests and Callbacks': Explain how section agents propose 0-2 diagrams after revision passes, exclude front matter, queue create_diagram_asset, and wait for callback tasks before inserting paths.

Conceptual diagram for 'Diagram Agent Memory': Explain why the diagram agent receives prior diagram context in its prompt and records each diagram's purpose, nodes, edges, layout, path, and distinctness rationale.

Conceptual diagram for 'Source Material and Section Payloads': Distinguish imported markdown source from generated LaTeX payloads and explain why section agents write only to section-specific TeX files.
Conceptual diagram for 'Proposals, Diffs, and Review': Explain how agent-authored edits become proposals, metadata, diffs, checkpoints, and reviewable changes instead of invisible mutations.
Conceptual diagram for 'Compilation and Repair Loops': Explain LaTeX assembly, build logs, compile diagnostics, section attribution, minimal repair tasks, and document-design feedback.

Conceptual diagram for 'Design Principles': Summarize the architecture's principles: durable state, explicit queues, specialist ownership, reversible edits, visible messages, and schema-first authoring.
Conceptual diagram for 'Limits and Failure Modes': Discuss repeated generic outputs, stale memories, over-eager diagrams, unsupported claims, prompt/context size, and the need for human review.

Conceptual diagram for 'Future Work': Identify improvements such as richer diagram planning, better global revision scheduling, stronger reference retrieval, UI observability, and more formal evaluation.
